Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 29 and juggling a lot of life responsibilities, so I couldn't stick to a consistent schedule. I'd often do intense bursts of studying when anxiety would hit me about falling behind, then nothing for days. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so I'd spend a lot of time working through practice problems offline rather than following any structured plan. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me recognize when I was spiraling, but organizing my time remained a struggle throughout.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was going on in my life and when deadlines were looming. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd realize an assignment was due and cram everything in. The anxiety made it hard to approach things steadily—I'd either avoid the work entirely or hyperfocus on it. I wish I could say I had regular study times, but that just wasn't my reality this semester.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember something was due or get an email notification and feel that familiar wave of dread. Sometimes I logged in just to check if I'd missed something important, which ironically made my anxiety worse. Occasionally I'd log in with good intentions to get ahead, but those moments were rare compared to the deadline-driven ones.
Q4: There were brief periods where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS in the mornings with my coffee. It happened after I started the mindfulness intervention and was trying to be more intentional about my approach. But honestly, that routine never really stuck for long—life would get in the way, or my anxiety would spike and I'd start avoiding it again. The LMS never truly became automatic for me the way I think it does for some students.
Q5: The video content was genuinely helpful because I could watch it multiple times and pause to take notes or try things myself. I watched over two hours of video content because I could go at my own pace and really absorb things kinesthetically by following along. The discussion posts were useful too when people shared practical examples—I engaged with about 8 posts because real-world applications clicked better for me than abstract theory.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just didn't match how I learn—I need to be doing something, not passively reading. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd sit unopened on my laptop. The discussion forums I also underused because my anxiety made me second-guess everything I wanted to post, so I'd write responses and then delete them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate practical application or when it directly connected to assessments. I needed to feel like I was actually learning something I could use, not just checking a box. If something felt too theoretical or disconnected from real outcomes, my motivation evaporated quickly. The hands-on exercises where I could practice and get feedback were the ones I actually valued.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap for my anxiety, honestly. Required ones I'd eventually force myself to do out of necessity, but optional ones created this weird guilt cycle where I knew I should do them but couldn't motivate myself, then felt bad about it. The pressure of required work was actually easier to manage because there was no ambiguity—I had to do it. Optional stuff just added to my mental load without clear payoff.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and materials, then work through them on my own time without being connected. I also used external YouTube tutorials and practice sites that explained concepts in different ways. My notes were all handwritten because writing things out helps me remember them kinesthetically. The LMS really only captured a fraction of what I was actually doing.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely engaged with learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd print out problem sets and work through them by hand at a coffee shop. I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times while practicing techniques. I also did a lot of studying with a friend who was taking similar courses—we'd work through concepts together, but none of that collaborative learning showed up anywhere in my digital footprint.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once to download it, then study from it for hours or even days afterward. My attendance looks terrible at 13% because I wasn't logging in live, but I was watching recorded content offline repeatedly. It was actually better for my anxiety to study this way—less pressure than being online where I felt like my every click was being tracked.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, and I started avoiding the course entirely because just thinking about logging in made my heart race. I was dealing with some personal stuff too, and my low resilience meant I couldn't bounce back quickly from setbacks. The mindfulness intervention helped somewhat, but there were definitely weeks where I completely disengaged because I was overwhelmed.
Q13: All of those factors hit me at various points. The workload across my 15 courses was genuinely overwhelming, and fatigue made everything harder. When I was confused about something, my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the LMS rather than seek help—which I know is counterproductive. Competing responsibilities as a 29-year-old with a life outside school definitely affected my engagement patterns too.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd log in and scroll through materials in a fog, not really absorbing anything because my mind was elsewhere or I was too anxious to focus. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while staring at the wall, technically watching but not learning anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to ease my guilt rather than actually studying.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible—I'd build them up in my head until they seemed insurmountable. I avoided discussion posts because I was terrified of saying something wrong publicly. Even when I knew procrastinating was making things worse, I couldn't break the cycle without the pressure of an imminent deadline.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have this pattern of avoidance followed by frantic last-minute effort. My assignment completion rate of 54% reflects all the times the anxiety won and I just... didn't submit things. When I did engage with deadlines, it was usually in the final hours, which wasn't great for the quality of my work or my mental health.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would surprise me and temporarily boost my motivation—I'd think maybe I could actually do this. But critical feedback, even when constructive, would send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid that area of the course. I'd delay looking at grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The course structure with its flexibility was both helpful and harmful for someone like me. I appreciated being able to work at my own pace, but without rigid structure, my tendency to drift and avoid took over. The 15 online courses I was taking all competed for my attention, and without mandatory attendance or check-ins, this course often lost out to more pressing demands.
Q19: The video content being available for rewatching made engagement easier—I could learn kinesthetically at my own pace. But the discussion requirements were harder for me because of social anxiety around posting publicly. The lack of structured check-ins meant I could drift for weeks without anyone noticing, which wasn't good for someone with my tendency to disengage when stressed.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. High login activity might mean someone is anxious and checking obsessively, or confused and searching for answers, rather than productively engaged. You might be clicking around trying to figure out what you're supposed to do, or avoiding the actual work by exploring other parts of the LMS. Activity doesn't equal output—sometimes it reflects struggle more than success.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My exam score was 82% despite terrible attendance because I was learning offline and in ways the LMS couldn't track. Some students just learn differently—we absorb material through methods that don't generate digital footprints. Low LMS activity might mean efficient studying elsewhere rather than disengagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login frequency and submission times, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or consistent engagement. But they completely miss the quality of attention, offline studying, emotional state while engaging, or learning that happens through external resources. They can't distinguish between anxious clicking and productive engagement, or between watching a video mindfully versus having it play in the background.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and feeling connected to the learning process. It's not just completing tasks—it's being mentally present and curious. For me, true engagement happened in those moments when I forgot my anxiety and got absorbed in solving a problem or understanding a concept. It's a state of mind, not a set of behaviors that can be easily tracked.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my constant battle with anxiety that shaped every interaction with the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the peer discussions, or the mindfulness practices I used to manage my stress. It also wouldn't show the genuine learning that happened despite my irregular patterns, or how surprised I was to actually do well on exams after feeling so disconnected all semester.
